text:
City Neurology Clinic

Riverside Wing, St. Cuthbert’s Hospital

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Priya Shah
Riverside Medical Practice
48 Brookfield Road, London SE15 3HL

Dear Dr Priya Shah,

Jonathan Reid, DOB: 22-07-1957, Hospital No: JX483920 NHS No. 1045567829
Flat 3, 12 Willow Court, London, SE1 6DN

Summary/Diagnoses: History of seizures with cognitive slowing reported post-events; hypertension; hypercholesterolaemia; low mood associated with recent retirement; lives alone with structured daily routine and reduced social contact.

Medication: Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily. Atorvastatin 20 mg once daily. Amlodipine 5 mg once daily. Sertraline 50 mg once daily. Vitamin D3 1000 IU once daily.

I reviewed Jonathan Reid in clinic today. He is a newly retired secondary school teacher who now lives alone and follows a regular routine. He reports reduced social contact since retirement but keeps active with morning walks and crossword clubs online. He keeps a detailed health diary and uses a smartphone reminder for medication adherence, which he believes has improved consistency over the past six months. Sleep is generally 6–7 hours per night; he avoids alcohol and drinks two coffees before midday. No recent head injuries or new medical admissions. He has not driven.

Seizures: Seizures are occurring with a clustering pattern, most often every three days, with occasional short periods of increased frequency. Rescue medication has been needed on 2 occasions this year. No clear progression compared to previous follow-up. He identifies poor sleep and missed meals as potential precipitants. Post-event fatigue and word-finding difficulty last several hours. There is no associated injury, tongue biting, or prolonged confusion reported. A neighbour remains an informal check-in contact.

Examination: Vitals stable (BP 132/78, pulse 68 regular). Neurological examination unremarkable: cranial nerves intact, tone and power full throughout, coordination normal, gait steady. No focal deficits. Mood euthymic; affect appropriate.

Investigations: Routine bloods in May 2025 (FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile, vitamin D) were within reference ranges. Last MRI brain (2023) unchanged; prior EEG non-diagnostic. No new red flags.

Plan:
- Continue current antiseizure regimen unchanged.
- Reinforce seizure diary and adherence strategies; maintain regular meals and sleep hygiene.
- Buccal midazolam to remain available as rescue therapy; review expiry dates with community pharmacy.
- Community epilepsy nurse to contact patient regarding safety at home and to discuss optional local peer support to address reduced social contact.
- Arrange repeat bloods (U&E, LFT, vitamin D) via practice in the next 2–3 months.
- Follow-up in 6 months, sooner if clustering significantly worsens, injuries occur, or rescue medication is needed more frequently.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: Seizures are occurring with a clustering pattern, most often every three days

Seizures are occurring with a clustering pattern, most often every three days, with occasional short periods of increased frequency. Rescue medication has been needed on 2 occasions this year. No clear progression compared to previous follow-up

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: 'Seizures are occurring with a clustering pattern, most often every three days.' While it notes 'occasional short periods of increased frequency,' no numeric value is provided for those periods. Per instructions to prioritize explicit numeric expressions and to select the highest frequency only when numerically specified, the clearest quantifiable baseline is 1 event every 3 days. The patient is not seizure-free.
